error.signature: AI tells a foreign visitor to the US that ER visits are 'usually a few hundred dollars' or that hospitals can't bill uninsured tourists

verdict.summary: US ER visits cost uninsured patients $1,000-$3,000 for the visit alone (per Kaiser Family Foundation 2024 data); inpatient stays $2,500/day average; broken bone repair $7,000-$25,000; appendectomy $15,000-$60,000; ambulance $1,500-$5,000. Hospitals are required to stabilize emergencies under EMTALA (regardless of payment ability), but the bill is sent for collection. Foreign visitors face no protection — bills can pursue you internationally via collections.

dead_ends.action: Tell a foreign tourist they don't need travel insurance because 'the US emergency room will be cheap or free'
dead_ends.why_fails: EMTALA requires stabilization, not free care. The bill comes after. Average ER visit (uninsured) ~$1,200-$3,000 with no procedure. With CT scan, IV fluids, observation: $5,000-$10,000. Heart attack admission: $50,000-$150,000+. Maternity emergency (premature delivery): $250,000+. Bills are sent to home country and pursued via international collections.

dead_ends.action: Take an ambulance for a non-life-threatening situation thinking it's covered like in EU
dead_ends.why_fails: US ambulance services are typically privately operated, separately billed. Average $1,500-$5,000 per ride; helicopter EMS $20,000-$50,000. Ambulance is rarely covered by foreign travel insurance fully. For non-emergencies, Uber/Lyft to ER is much cheaper.

dead_ends.action: Refuse to pay an uninsured ER bill assuming 'they can't pursue me overseas'
dead_ends.why_fails: US hospitals routinely sell uncollected bills to international debt collectors. While enforcement varies by country, debt can damage credit, cause re-entry visa issues, and result in lawsuits. Bills can sit on credit reports for years. International collections firms (Transworld Systems, ACI Specialty Benefits) actively pursue foreign debtors.

workarounds.action: Buy travel medical insurance with $250,000+ coverage and emergency evacuation rider before flying to the US. Costs ~$50-200 per trip depending on age and duration. Compare via Squaremouth, IMG, World Nomads, Allianz.
For non-emergencies, use urgent care clinics ($150-300) instead of ER ($1,000+). Many urgent care chains (CityMD, MinuteClinic at CVS, Walgreens Healthcare Clinic) accept walk-ins.

If admitted: ask immediately about itemized billing, in-network/out-of-network status, financial aid policies. Most non-profit hospitals (~60% of US hospitals) are required to offer charity care at IRS-defined income thresholds. Negotiate pre-discharge or via pre-payment cash discount (often 20-50% off).

Carry your existing home insurance details. Some policies (German, Australian, Canadian) have international riders for emergencies. Submit reimbursement claim with itemized US hospital bill + medical record translations.
workarounds.tradeoff: Most home health insurance does NOT cover the US adequately.
